transcript.content: So you're saying recursion is a function that calls itself, but I don't see how that's really different from a loop. Doesn't it just keep repeating until it stops, like a while loop would? Can you show me an example where recursion actually does something a loop can't, because right now I'm not seeing what the big deal is.

Run factorial(3) with no base case. 3 calls 2, calls 1, calls 0, calls -1, calls -2... it never stops. It doesn't 'finish' — it crashes. A loop's condition is checked at the top; in recursion, the base case is the condition. Take it away and there's nothing to stop the descent.

But that's just because you're not checking if the number is less than 0 before making the recursive call, right? A loop would also crash if you didn't have a condition to stop it. So, if I just added a check to make sure the number is not less than 0 before calling the function again, wouldn't that be the same as having a base case? Can you explain why that's not enough?

You're right that both run forever. But they fail differently, and that's the point. An infinite while loop runs forever using constant memory — it just spins. Infinite recursion crashes, and fast, because every call gets its own frame on the call stack: its own copy of n, its own return address. factorial(3) with no base case doesn't spin — it piles up frames until the stack is full and the program dies with a stack overflow. A loop reuses one set of variables. Recursion stacks up a new set every call. That's why the base case isn't a loop condition dressed up — it's the only thing keeping the stack from growing without bound.

So you're saying the difference is how they use memory, and that recursion can crash because it keeps adding new frames to the stack, whereas a loop just reuses the same space. But couldn't I just increase the stack size to avoid the overflow, or use some other way to manage the memory? That doesn't seem like a fundamental difference between loops and recursion, just a practical limitation. Can you show me how this affects the actual code, like how the base case prevents the stack from growing out of control?

final_belief_state.evidence: Infinite recursion crashes, and fast, because every call gets its own frame on the call stack: its own copy of n, its own return address... A loop reuses one set of variables. Recursion stacks up a new set every call. That's why the base case isn't a loop condition dressed up — it's the only thing keeping the stack from growing without bound.
